summary: Patrick McKenzie describes being approached by Bits du Jour, a daily software deal site, to feature Bingo Card Creator during their back-to-school week at a 40% discount. He explains why he generally avoids affiliate programs — the small per-sale incentive doesn't motivate affiliates to do quality marketing — but was persuaded by their low-friction pitch and trust-based approach. He sets modest expectations of roughly 2 sales given his niche product, but agrees to the experiment since it costs him almost nothing. He commits to sharing the results publicly.
key_points: The Bits du Jour model applies Woot's one-deal-per-day urgency concept to software, taking an affiliate commission while letting authors keep their own shopping cart and payment infrastructure
McKenzie's general anti-affiliate rationale: a $5 per-sale incentive doesn't motivate quality promotion, and he'd rather spend that money on AdWords where he controls the entire funnel
The pitch succeeded because it required minimal effort (under 10 minutes of setup), used trust-based terms, and carried essentially zero downside risk
Niche products face a natural ceiling on deal sites — unless the audience happens to include hundreds of teachers, a daily deal site's general traffic won't convert well for specialized software
McKenzie frames business decisions as small experiments with bounded downside rather than strategic commitments
money_quotes: I have a $23.93 incentive to sell one marginal copy of my software. Any given affiliate has, say, a $5 incentive. $5 doesn't motivate someone to put any investment or thought into actually getting my software in front of people who need it.
I'd much rather spend the affiliate's commission on AdWords, where I have total control of the experience from beginning to end and only have to deal with myself.
Nothing motivates people like the words 'I trust you'. Particularly when its 'I trust that we will have such a successful relationship that you will choose to pay me at the end of it'.
I'm personally betting on 2, which means about $23 for me and $6 to Bits du Jour — I actually mentioned that to them and they said they were happy to experiment.
That is the sort of transparency that many Internet companies brag about but few actually achieve.
key_insight: Affiliate programs are generally a bad deal for niche software because the small per-sale commission doesn't motivate quality promotion, but low-effort experiments with bounded downside are always worth trying.
